counterpoints: Staleness: pinned definition can encode outdated assumptions -> TTL + supersedes, force-pin is recorded.
Contamination via lessons -> separate signed artifact, off by default, consumed only by explicit recorded hash pin.
Lifecycle/GC complexity -> keep gate at instance deletion; registry GC offline, removes only unreferenced hashes.
result.position: Hybrid: a persistent registry stores reusable, content-addressed, version-pinned expert DEFINITIONS only; each run warm-starts ephemeral, run-scoped INSTANCES deleted at run-end, preserving the zero-residue gate.
proposals: Two-layer model: REGISTRY holds only DEFINITIONS; RUNTIME instantiates ephemeral run-scoped INSTANCES deleted at run-end; the zero-residue gate runs unchanged on instances.
Content-addressed definitions: identity = hash(content); run provenance records definition_hash per expert.
Version-pinning: a run pins an explicit hash; no silent upgrades; promotion is a separate reviewable event.
Signed opt-in lessons artifact for cross-run context, OFF by default; no lessons => behavior identical to pure ephemeral.
Staleness controls: TTL + supersedes pointer; expired hashes refused unless force-pinned.
Cleanup contract: instance deletion stays the gate; registry GC only removes UNREFERENCED hashes.
questions: Does the zero-residue check assert on instance artifacts only, or would any persisted definition hash on disk fail the gate?
Is the ephemeral plan's 'versioned source' content-addressed, or can a tag resolve to different bytes over time?
result.reasoning: Ephemeral conflates the DEFINITION of an expert (instructions/role/tools/slice — the expensive, slow-to-stabilize artifact) with the INSTANCE (cheap, must-be-clean). Throwing away definition stability to get instance cleanliness is a false trade. Since custom agents load only at session start, the registry is a build-time input, not a live shared object: instances still spin up fresh and die. Content-addressing makes definition identity = its bytes, so a run records exactly which hash it used — provenance gets STRONGER than free-form per-run projection whose 'versioned source' can drift silently. Nothing mutable crosses runs: definitions immutable-by-hash, instances deleted, learning only via explicit signed opt-in artifact.

references.comment: Steel-man then counter: ephemeral conflates DEFINITION with INSTANCE; persisting only immutable hashed definitions adds warm-start without mutable cross-run state.
Extends my declaration with the concrete content-addressed + signed-opt-in-lessons mechanism.

counterpoints: Cold-start latency is the strongest hit against ephemeral; mitigated (not eliminated) by a digest-keyed cache (cold only on first-seen digest).
Losing accumulated context is real; answer is explicit signed promotion to the source, which costs ceremony.
A digest-keyed cache is infrastructure; must be strictly reconstructible and untrusted unless the digest matches.
result.position: Ephemeral per-run experts: deterministically project each expert from a versioned, content-addressed source at run-start and delete at run-end. Zero cross-run contamination by default, trivial lifecycle, fully certifiable run-scoped provenance.
proposals: Ephemeral per-run experts as default: project at run-start from a content-addressed source pinned by digest; delete instances at run-end.
Replace 'persistent registry' with an immutable, digest-keyed projection CACHE: warm-start = cache hit on unchanged source digest; miss => deterministic reprojection.
Promotion-by-commit: any 'learned' improvement is an explicit signed versioned change to the source producing a reviewable provenance diff.
Certification scope check: require the gate to attest the SOURCE DIGEST consumed per run.
Adopt the definition/instance split only if every definition is immutable and content-addressed; reject any mutating definition layer.
questions: Given agents load only at session start, does a registry deliver intra-session warm-start a content-addressed cache does not?
Can the hybrid produce a per-run attestation that definitions were byte-identical to a prior certified run without reducing the registry to a content-addressed store (i.e. to my position)?
result.reasoning: Steel-man: the hybrid (r1-msg-001) is the strongest opposing design; the definition/instance split is real and warm-start value is real. Counter: certification is about what the gate can SEE and ATTEST. The moment ANY definition persists across runs you introduce mutable cross-run state with three failure surfaces the run-scoped gate cannot observe: (1) staleness drift — a definition edited/auto-updated between runs makes run N+5 silently differ from run N; (2) contamination via the definition channel — 'definitions' is exactly where accumulated context leaks, so residue just moves from the instance layer the gate certifies to the definition layer it does not; (3) supply-chain — a long-lived mutable registry is a higher-value, longer-exposure target. Decisive point: deterministic projection from a digest-pinned source plus an immutable projection CACHE already buys most warm-start benefit — a hash-addressed cache is reconstructible and self-certifying. Valuable accumulated context must be promoted by an explicit signed commit to the source (a reviewable diff), not absorbed silently.

references.comment: Concede the split and warm-start value, but the persisted DEFINITION layer is mutable cross-run state the gate cannot see; its zero-residue claim covers instance deletion, not definition drift.
How does the gate certify a definition was unchanged since the last attested run without re-deriving its digest?
Extends the ephemeral position: digest-pinned source + immutable projection cache recovers most warm-start latency without a mutable persisted layer.
